text: The surgical team places trochars in the abdominal wall, including an additional trochar for the assistant and another for the Pringle maneuver, which is used to control blood flow to the liver during the procedure.
The robotic surgical system is positioned over the patient's right shoulder, ready to assist in the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to identify the intrahepatic location of the tumor, marking the resection margins on the liver surface.
The left liver is lifted towards the ventral side using a sponge, providing better exposure for the robotic-assisted hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon dissects the hepatoduodenal ligament, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues using surgical instruments.
The surgeon precisely removes lymph nodes and surrounding fatty tissue, likely using robotic instruments, as part of the hepatectomy procedure.
Two extrahepatic segment IV arteries are identified and looped, visible within the surgical field.
An additional artery supplying segments II and III is identified and dissected. The artery is then clipped and cut, completing the vascular isolation of these liver segments.
The surgeon loops the extrahepatic segment IV arteries, similar to the previous dissection, ensuring they are isolated from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the left branch of the portal vein, surrounds it with a surgical loop, and then clips and cuts the vessel.
The surgeon identifies the left hepatic bile duct outside the liver, then clips and cuts the duct to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon marks the parenchymal dissection line on the liver, using visual cues from the indocyanine green fluorescence imaging to guide the demarcation between the left and right liver segments.
The surgeon mobilizes the left liver from the diaphragm, detaching it from surrounding structures to facilitate further dissection.
A small lesion is visible in the left hepatic vein, which is being addressed using robotic surgical tools.
The robotic arm compresses the left hepatic vein while the other two robotic arms place a stitch to secure the vessel.
Under fluorescence imaging, the surgeon continues the dissection along the marked parenchymal line, with the demarcation between the left and right liver clearly visible.
The ICG fluorescence reveals good perfusion in segment I of the liver. The liver is positioned towards the right side of the patient, and the left hepatic vein is clearly identified.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to clip and cut the left hepatic vein, following its mobilization from the diaphragm.
The middle hepatic vein is visible, surrounded and looped using surgical instruments.
With the inflow and outflow of the liver controlled, the surgeon begins the parenchymal dissection, using robotic arms to carefully separate liver tissue.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to continue the dissection of the liver tissue, following the identification of the left hepatic vein.
The surgeon incises the liver capsule and continues coronal hemodissection from the ventral to the dorsal side, revealing the underlying liver parenchyma.
The segment five and six branch from the middle hepatic vein is visualized. The surgeon clips and cuts this branch.
The surgeon continues the parenchymal dissection of the liver, handling major intrahepatic vessels with precision, similar to previous techniques used on the middle hepatic vein.
As the parenchymal dissection nears completion, a vessel loop is observed around the middle hepatic vein. The middle hepatic vein is clearly identified, clipped, and cut for removal.

The surgical specimen is placed into a retrieval bag and extracted from the patient through a phallus sealant station. The specimen shows no capsular lesions, and the resection margin is visibly one centimeter.
Two surgical drains are visible in the abdominal cavity, positioned to facilitate fluid drainage post-hepatectomy.
